option: Warm Off-White + Ink (#F7F4EE base, #1A1A17 text, single warm accent)
best_for: Editorial, portfolio, and brand-forward SaaS sites wanting a warm, print-inspired feel instead of the default cold gray-on-white
avoid_when: A product needing high information density (dashboards) where warm off-white can reduce perceived contrast at small sizes
tradeoffs: Feels considered and less 'default Tailwind gray' at zero extra cost, but needs the accent color chosen deliberately since it carries the palette's entire personality

option: Cool Slate Neutrals (#0F172A through #F8FAFC, one saturated accent)
best_for: Technical/developer-facing products wanting a precise, structured feel
avoid_when: A brand wanting warmth or approachability — cool slate reads clinical/technical by design
tradeoffs: A well-worn but reliable palette family; distinctiveness has to come from the single accent choice and typography, not the neutrals themselves

option: Brutalist Black & White + Acid Accent (#000000, #FFFFFF, one acid tone like #D4FF00)
best_for: Brands wanting to signal confidence/boldness — portfolios, creative agencies, product launches wanting to stand out from soft SaaS defaults
avoid_when: Healthcare, finance, or any context where the audience expects calm/trustworthy over bold
tradeoffs: Extremely high contrast and memorable, but has zero room for subtlety — every other design decision (type, spacing) has to be equally confident or the boldness reads as unfinished rather than intentional

option: Deep Ink + Single Warm Accent (#12100E base, #FFFFFF text, #E8734A accent)
best_for: Premium/editorial products wanting a dark, confident feel without going full brutalist
avoid_when: Content-heavy pages where extended dark-mode reading needs a softer near-black instead of true black
tradeoffs: Reads premium and considered; near-black instead of pure black is easier on the eyes for longer reading sessions than #000000

option: Muted Sage & Clay (#8A9A8E, #C4A484, #F5F1EA base)
best_for: Wellness, sustainability, and lifestyle brands wanting an organic, calm feel distinct from typical SaaS blue
avoid_when: A product needing to feel technical, fast, or high-energy — muted tones actively work against urgency
tradeoffs: Distinctive specifically because most competitors default to blue — the palette itself becomes a differentiator in a blue-saturated category

option: Desaturated Pastel Tech (#B8C5D6, #D6C5B8, #E8E4DE base)
best_for: Consumer tech products wanting to feel friendly/approachable rather than corporate — the 'soft tech' aesthetic
avoid_when: B2B enterprise sales contexts where desaturated pastels can undercut a 'serious tool' positioning
tradeoffs: Approachable and calm, but this exact aesthetic direction became common in consumer tech circa 2023-2025 — pair with a distinctive type choice (frontend-distinctiveness.md) so the palette alone doesn't read as templated

option: Deep Jewel Tones (#5B2C6F emerald-adjacent purple, #1E5F4E deep teal, #F4C430 gold accent)
best_for: Luxury, hospitality, and premium consumer brands wanting richness without going to primary/neon colors
avoid_when: Minimalist or utility-focused products where jewel tones would read as decorative rather than functional
tradeoffs: Signals richness and craft, but needs careful contrast checking (WCAG, `QA027`) since jewel tones can have deceptively low contrast against each other at a glance

option: Electric Gradient Duo (e.g. #6366F1 to #EC4899, used sparingly on hero/CTA surfaces only)
best_for: A single high-impact hero moment or CTA — not the whole interface
avoid_when: Applying the gradient everywhere — a gradient used as the entire palette rather than a targeted accent reads as generic 'AI startup' aesthetic
tradeoffs: Gradients are simultaneously one of the most overused AND most effective techniques when scoped correctly — the distinction is restraint: one hero section, not every button, not every card

option: Single-Hue Ramp (one hue, 8-10 lightness steps, no second color except semantic red/green)
best_for: Data-dense products (dashboards, admin panels) where visual noise from multiple hues actively hurts scanability
avoid_when: Marketing/brand pages needing more personality than a single hue can carry alone
tradeoffs: Forces genuine information-hierarchy discipline since lightness/weight must do the work color usually does — often produces cleaner UIs than multi-color palettes by removing a degree of freedom that's easy to misuse

option: Duotone (two hues only: one neutral-adjacent, one accent, used consistently)
best_for: Portfolio and product-marketing sites wanting a distinctive, consistent visual signature across every page
avoid_when: Complex apps needing many semantic colors (status, category, priority) — duotone constrains the palette below what's needed there
tradeoffs: The constraint itself becomes the brand signature — recognizable specifically because it's unusual to commit to only two hues this strictly

option: Status Colors Distinct From Brand Accent (never reuse the primary brand color for success/error/warning)
best_for: Every product with any status/feedback UI — success, warning, error, and info states get their own dedicated colors, separate from whatever the brand's primary accent is
avoid_when: A single-status product with no real success/error/warning distinction needed — rare, but worth confirming before adding unused semantic tokens
tradeoffs: Reusing the brand accent for 'success' means a user can't distinguish 'this is our brand color' from 'this succeeded' — dedicated semantic colors remove that ambiguity entirely

option: Color Never the Only Signal (pair with icon/text/pattern)
best_for: Every status/state indicator — this is QA034 applied to color-system design specifically: color reinforces meaning, it doesn't solely carry it
avoid_when: Never applicable to skip — this is a WCAG requirement (1.4.1), not a stylistic choice
tradeoffs: A colorblind user (roughly 1 in 12 men) must be able to tell success from error without relying on hue alone — icon shape or text label alongside color closes this gap entirely

option: Near-Black, Not Pure Black, for Dark Mode Backgrounds (#0A0A0B-#151517 range)
best_for: Every dark mode implementation — pure #000000 backgrounds create harsh contrast against white text and can cause halation/glow effects for some users
avoid_when: A deliberately extreme high-contrast aesthetic (the brutalist palette, UX003) where pure black is the intended effect
tradeoffs: Near-black reduces eye strain on OLED and LCD alike while still reading as 'dark mode,' and gives more room for elevation/depth via slightly lighter surface layers than pure black allows

option: Desaturate Accent Colors for Dark Backgrounds, Don't Reuse Light-Mode Values
best_for: Every dark mode implementation with a saturated brand accent — a fully saturated color that works on white can vibrate/glow uncomfortably against near-black
avoid_when: A muted/desaturated palette to begin with (UX005/UX006) where light-mode accents are already dark-mode-safe
tradeoffs: Directly porting light-mode accent values to dark mode is a common, visible tell of an unfinished dark mode — slightly desaturating and often slightly lightening the accent for dark backgrounds reads as intentional rather than inverted

option: Fintech: Deep Blue/Navy + Confident Green (avoid red except for real loss/error states)
best_for: Financial products wanting to signal trust and stability through established color association
avoid_when: A fintech brand deliberately positioning against stodgy incumbents — an unconventional palette can itself be the differentiation strategy there
tradeoffs: Navy/green carries strong pre-existing trust associations in financial contexts; deviating from it is a legitimate choice but should be deliberate, not accidental

option: Healthcare: Soft Blue/Teal + High-Legibility Neutral (avoid clinical stark white/gray alone)
best_for: Healthcare and wellness products needing to feel both credible and calming, not sterile
avoid_when: A healthcare brand targeting clinicians/professionals rather than patients — that audience often responds better to the more clinical/technical palette (UX002)
tradeoffs: Soft blue/teal reads as calming without sacrificing the credibility signal blue carries broadly across healthcare contexts

option: One Accent Color, Used Consistently for Every Interactive Element
best_for: Any product wanting a coherent, learnable interface — the accent color becomes a reliable signal for 'this is clickable/interactive'
avoid_when: A product with genuinely distinct interaction categories needing visual differentiation (e.g. destructive vs. primary actions) — those still get their own semantic colors (UX011), the single-accent rule applies to primary/default interactive elements
tradeoffs: Consistency here does real usability work: once a user learns 'the accent color means interactive,' that pattern holds everywhere, reducing cognitive load across the whole product

option: Accent Color Chosen to Contrast the Category Norm, Not Follow It
best_for: Establishing distinctiveness in a crowded category — if every competitor uses blue, a deliberately different accent (not necessarily bold, just different) is free differentiation
avoid_when: A regulated/trust-dependent category where the color convention (e.g. fintech blue) carries real trust signaling that's risky to deviate from without strong justification
tradeoffs: This is the single highest-leverage, lowest-cost distinctiveness lever available — before reaching for unusual layouts or typography, check whether the palette itself is just doing what every competitor already does

option: Subtle Grain/Noise Texture Over Flat Color Fields
best_for: Editorial and premium-feeling brand surfaces wanting to avoid the perfectly flat, slightly sterile look of pure flat design
avoid_when: Data-dense UI (dashboards, tables) where texture would add visual noise without benefit
tradeoffs: A small amount of grain (1-3% opacity noise) breaks up perfectly flat color fields in a way that reads as intentional/crafted rather than a rendering artifact — a specific, cheap technique for making flat design feel less generic

option: Layered Soft Shadows Over Hard Borders for Elevation
best_for: Product surfaces wanting a softer, more tactile feel than sharp 1px borders everywhere
avoid_when: A brutalist or sharp-edged aesthetic (UX003) where hard borders are the intended effect
tradeoffs: Multiple soft, low-opacity shadow layers (rather than one hard shadow or a border) read as more considered and less like a default component library's out-of-the-box card style
